name: Selenium
name_origin: Named after 'selene', the Greek word for the Moon, by Jons Jacob Berzelius in 1817 to complement the previously known element tellurium (named after the Earth).

discovery.discoverer: Jons Jacob Berzelius and Johan Gottlieb Gahn

occurrence.abundance_summary: Selenium is rare in Earth's crust at roughly 0.05 ppm. It is recovered almost entirely as a by-product of copper refining (anode slimes); major producers include Japan, Germany, Belgium, and Russia.
states: rare minerals such as clausthalite (PbSe) and crooksite
trace in soils and certain plants

compounds.name: Selenium dioxide
compounds.summary: White solid that sublimes readily; an important reagent in organic synthesis for the oxidation of allylic and benzylic positions.

compounds.name: Selenic acid
compounds.summary: Strong, hot-concentrated oxidizing acid analogous to sulfuric acid; capable of dissolving gold and used in selenate-salt synthesis.

compounds.name: Hydrogen selenide
compounds.summary: Colorless, highly toxic gas with an offensive odor; used as a precursor in the deposition of selenide semiconductors.

compounds.name: Cadmium selenide
compounds.summary: Direct-bandgap semiconductor used in photoconductors and as the basis of many quantum-dot displays and biomedical fluorophores.

compounds.name: Sodium selenite
compounds.summary: Soluble salt used as a dietary supplement source of selenium and as an additive in glassmaking.

uses: Decolorizing and red-coloring agent in glass manufacturing
Photoreceptor drums in older xerographic copiers and laser printers
Photovoltaic and photoconductor cells (including CIGS thin-film solar cells)
Dietary supplement and animal-feed additive (essential micronutrient)
Alloying additive that improves machinability of steel and copper
reactivity_summary: Selenium exists in several allotropes including red amorphous, gray (metallic) trigonal, and red monoclinic forms. It is stable in air at room temperature and burns with a blue flame to give SeO2. Selenium reacts with most metals to form selenides, and with strong oxidizing acids to form selenous and selenic acids. Its photoconductivity (resistance drops sharply on illumination) underpins many of its electronic applications.
isotopes_summary: Selenium has six naturally occurring isotopes: 74Se (~0.89%), 76Se (~9.37%), 77Se (~7.63%), 78Se (~23.77%), 80Se (~49.61%), and 82Se (~8.73%). 82Se is observationally stable but undergoes very slow double beta decay and is studied in neutrinoless-double-beta-decay experiments. The longest-lived radioisotope is 79Se (half-life ~3.27 x 10^5 years).

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
